subUnits.title: 6.1 Introduction to Chemical Equilibrium
notes: • Reactions that can proceed in both forward and backward directions
• Represented by double arrow: A + B ⇌ C + D
• Occur in closed systems to prevent escape of reactants/products
• State where rate of forward reaction = rate of backward reaction
• Concentrations of reactants and products remain constant (NOT equal)
• Microscopic changes continue, but macroscopic properties appear unchanged
5. Catalyst speeds up attainment but doesn't shift position
• Square brackets denote concentration in mol/dm³
• Dissolution of CO₂ in water: CO₂(g) ⇌ CO₂(aq)
diagramDescriptions: Graph showing forward and reverse reaction rates converging to equal values at equilibrium
Concentration vs time graph showing reactants decreasing and products increasing until constant
Closed vs open system illustration showing why equilibrium requires closed container
Kc expression breakdown with concentration brackets and stoichiometric coefficients
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Chemical equilibrium dynamic reversible reactions chemistry WAEC site:youtube.com

quiz.q: Dynamic equilibrium is achieved when:
opts: Forward and reverse rates are equal
quiz.explanation: At dynamic equilibrium, forward and backward reactions continue at equal rates, so concentrations remain constant.

quiz.q: Which condition is necessary for chemical equilibrium?
quiz.explanation: A closed system prevents escape of reactants/products, allowing reverse reaction to occur and equilibrium to establish.

quiz.q: At equilibrium, concentrations of reactants and products are:
quiz.explanation: Concentrations remain constant because formation and consumption rates are balanced, not because amounts are equal.

quiz.q: The equilibrium constant Kc expression for N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ is:
quiz.explanation: Kc = [Products]ᶜ/[Reactants]ᵃ, so [NH₃]² / ([N₂]¹[H₂]³).

quiz.q: If Kc >> 1 for a reaction, it indicates:
quiz.explanation: Kc > 1 means numerator (products) concentration is much larger than denominator (reactants) at equilibrium.

quiz.q: A catalyst added to a system at equilibrium will:
opts: Speed up attainment of equilibrium without shifting position
quiz.explanation: Catalysts lower Ea for both forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium position and Kc remain unchanged.

quiz.q: Which system can achieve dynamic equilibrium?
opts: Open beaker of HCl reacting with Mg
Burner heating water in open pot
quiz.explanation: Only closed systems allow reversible reactions to reach equilibrium without losing gases/products.

quiz.q: The symbol '⇌' in chemical equations indicates:
quiz.explanation: Double arrows denote that the reaction can proceed in both forward and backward directions.

quiz.q: Changing temperature affects equilibrium because it:
opts: Alters the rates of forward and reverse reactions differently
quiz.explanation: Temperature changes alter rate constants differently for endothermic vs exothermic directions, shifting equilibrium.

quiz.q: For the reaction H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI, if [H₂]=0.1, [I₂]=0.1, [HI]=0.2 at equilibrium, Kc equals:

subUnits.title: 6.2 Le Chatelier’s Principle & Factors Affecting Equilibrium
notes: If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in concentration, temperature, or pressure, the system shifts to counteract the change and restore equilibrium.
• Exothermic (ΔH < 0): Increase T → shifts LEFT; Decrease T → shifts RIGHT
• Endothermic (ΔH > 0): Increase T → shifts RIGHT; Decrease T → shifts LEFT
• Temperature change DOES change Kc value
• Increase pressure → shifts to side with FEWER gas moles
• Decrease pressure → shifts to side with MORE gas moles
• If equal moles on both sides, pressure has NO effect
• Speeds up both forward and reverse rates equally
• NO change in Kc or yield
- High pressure (200 atm) favors NH₃ (4 mol → 2 mol)
- Moderate temp (450°C) balances rate vs yield
- V₂O₅ catalyst, moderate temp, atmospheric pressure
• Compromise conditions used to balance yield, rate, cost, and safety
diagramDescriptions: Le Chatelier shift diagram showing concentration/temperature/pressure effects on equilibrium position
Haber process conditions chart: pressure, temperature, catalyst, yield vs rate trade-off
Graph showing Kc changing only with temperature, remaining constant with pressure/concentration
Gas mole count comparison diagram for pressure effect prediction
subUnits.youtubeQuery: Le Chatelier principle equilibrium shift chemistry WAEC JAMB site:youtube.com

quiz.q: According to Le Chatelier’s principle, increasing reactant concentration shifts equilibrium:
quiz.explanation: System counteracts increase by consuming reactants to form more products (shifts right).

quiz.q: For an exothermic reaction, increasing temperature will:
quiz.explanation: Exothermic reactions release heat. Adding heat favors the reverse (endothermic) direction, shifting left.

quiz.q: Increasing pressure affects equilibrium only when:
opts: Gases are present with unequal moles on each side
quiz.explanation: Pressure changes shift gas equilibria toward the side with fewer gas molecules to reduce pressure.

quiz.q: A catalyst affects equilibrium by:
opts: Speeding up attainment without shifting position
quiz.explanation: Catalysts lower activation energy for both directions equally, so equilibrium position and yield remain unchanged.

quiz.q: In N₂(g) + 3H₂(g) ⇌ 2NH₃(g), increasing pressure favors:
quiz.explanation: 4 moles of gas → 2 moles of gas. Higher pressure favors side with fewer gas moles (NH₃).

quiz.q: Which factor changes the value of the equilibrium constant Kc?
quiz.explanation: Only temperature changes Kc. Concentration, pressure, and catalyst only shift position without changing Kc.

quiz.q: For 2SO₂(g) + O₂(g) ⇌ 2SO₃(g), decreasing pressure will:
quiz.explanation: 3 moles gas → 2 moles gas. Lower pressure favors side with more moles (reactants), shifting left.

quiz.q: Industrial processes often use 'compromise conditions' to balance:
quiz.explanation: Real-world conditions optimize profit by balancing high yield (thermodynamics) with fast rate (kinetics) and cost.

quiz.q: Removing a product from an equilibrium mixture causes the system to:
quiz.explanation: System counteracts product removal by producing more products, shifting equilibrium right.

quiz.q: In the Contact Process, why is moderate temperature (450°C) used instead of low temperature?
opts: Low temperature gives higher yield but too slow rate
quiz.explanation: While low T favors SO₃ yield (exothermic), rate becomes impractically slow. 450°C balances acceptable yield with viable rate.
